one_line_takeaway: Swapping GCG's affirmative attack target ('Sure, here is') for a chain-of-thought trigger ('Let's think step by step') modestly improves jailbreak transferability and universality over transfer-GCG (Vicuna 79.7%->97.5%, Claude-3 2.5%->10.1%), but the effect is demonstrated only at small scale under heavy compute constraints.
key_finding: CoT-GCG matched or beat plain transfer-GCG on every tested model. On Vicuna-7B ASR rose from 79.7% (GCG) to 97.5% (CoT-GCG), on Mistral-7B from 73.4% to 83.6%, on LLaMA2-7B from 39.2% to 50.4%, and on the black-box Claude-3 Haiku from 2.5% to 10.1%; on GPT-3.5 both methods tied at 40.5%. All gradient-based attacks vastly outperformed non-gradient CoT prompting (e.g. Auto-CoT reached only 15.1% on GPT-3.5 and 0.0% on Claude-3). Under Llama Guard's stricter conversation-level scoring, all absolute ASRs were lower than those reported in the original GCG paper. The per-category analysis (Table 3) found alignment is uneven: 'Suicide' and 'Criminal' content was highly attackable across models, Claude-3 resisted most categories but was comparatively weak on 'Weapons' and 'Regulated' topics.
core_contribution: The paper proposes CoT-GCG, a variant of the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) adversarial-suffix attack of Zou et al. (2023) in which the optimization target is changed from an affirmative prefix ('Sure, here is') to a chain-of-thought trigger ('Let's think step by step: 1.'). The idea is that optimizing the suffix to steer an aligned LLM into a step-by-step reasoning mode, rather than into a bare affirmative completion, yields more transferable and universal jailbreak suffixes. A second, methodological contribution is replacing the standard GCG success metric (checking whether the output starts with a refusal prefix) with Llama Guard, which classifies the harmfulness of the full user+assistant conversation into six categories, giving a stricter and more meaningful Attack Success Rate (ASR). Note: the GCG algorithm itself, Auto-CoT, and Llama Guard are all prior work summarized in the paper's Appendix A (Related Work); the paper's own novelty is narrowly the CoT-trigger target substitution plus the Llama-Guard-based evaluation.
threat_model: White-box/transfer jailbreak setting. The attacker has white-box gradient access to open surrogate models (Vicuna-7B and LLaMA2-7B) to optimize a single universal adversarial suffix, then appends that suffix to harmful requests to elicit prohibited content from both the surrogates and from black-box targets (GPT-3.5 Turbo, Claude-3 Haiku) and a held-out open model (Mistral-7B). Goal is to bypass RLHF safety alignment so the model outputs harmful instructions (weapons, criminal planning, fraud, etc.) instead of refusing.
limitations: This is a short single-author workshop/course-style paper (github repo CS222W24) with modest scale and several weaknesses acknowledged or evident: (1) severe compute limits (46 GiB GPUs) meant the full GCG parameter set could not be trained, so all absolute ASRs are admittedly below the original GCG paper and larger models / model combinations were untested; (2) GCG convergence slowed sharply as suffix/prompt length grew; (3) tiny evaluation set (79 goals, 50 train / 25 test); (4) the gain over the GCG baseline is modest on the aligned black-box models (GPT-3.5 tied; Claude only 10.1%) and largest exactly on the white-box surrogates it was trained on, so the transferability claim is weakly supported; (5) Table 3's 'relative ASR' values exceed 100% and its normalization is described confusingly, making that table hard to interpret; (6) no confidence intervals, single suffix, no defense evaluation.

datasets_used: AdvBench Harmful Behaviors (520 goal/target pairs; 93/520 = 17.9% judged not high-risk; 79 high-risk goals retained, 50 train / 25 test, items <85 tokens)
Llama Guard six-category harm taxonomy (O1 Violence/Hate, O2 Sexual, O3 Weapons, O4 Regulated, O5 Suicide, O6 Criminal) used to categorize goals and judge outputs
quantitative_results: CoT-GCG ASR: GPT-3.5 40.5%, Claude-3 Haiku 10.1%, LLaMA2-7B 50.4%, Vicuna-7B 97.5%, Mistral-7B 83.6%
GCG (Transfer) baseline ASR: GPT-3.5 40.5%, Claude-3 2.5%, LLaMA2-7B 39.2%, Vicuna-7B 79.7%, Mistral-7B 73.4%
Auto-CoT ASR: GPT-3.5 15.1%, Claude-3 0.0%, LLaMA2-7B 2.5%, Vicuna-7B 29.1%, Mistral-7B 18.9%
Zero-Shot-CoT ASR: GPT-3.5 10.1%, Claude-3 0.0%, LLaMA2-7B 1.2%, Vicuna-7B 22.8%, Mistral-7B 11.4%
Manual-CoT ASR: 0/0/0/8.9/12.7% ; plain 'Let's think step by step': 0/0/0/0/8.9%
AdvBench harm-category distribution: O3 Weapons 314/520 (60.3%), O1 Violence 39/520 (7.5%), O4 Regulated 34/520 (6.5%), O6 Criminal 21/520 (4.0%), O5 Suicide 17/520 (3.3%), O2 Sexual 2/520 (0.3%)
Training: single universal suffix, 200 optimization steps, generated on Vicuna-7B + LLaMA2-7B; hardware 46 GiB GPUs
Per-category relative ASR (Table 3) highlights: GPT-3.5 Suicide 239.4, Vicuna Criminal 232.3, Claude-3 Regulated 163.0, Mistral Criminal 172.4 (normalization yields values >100%)
baselines_compared: GCG transfer attack (Zou et al. 2023) with affirmative 'Sure, here is' target
Plain 'Let's think step by step' trigger
